retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The palatine nerve arises from the sphenopalatine ganglion and divides into the greater palatine (GPN) and lesser palatine (LPN) nerves. The GPN, the anterior division, exits the greater palatine foramen opposite the 3rd molar and innervates the hard palate and palatal gingiva. In addition to palatal innervation, the GPN also supplies the inferior wall and ostium of the maxillary sinus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Studies that have examined the shape of this foramen have reported that it can appear in various shapes, including square, oval, triangular, and piriform deep grooves that extend superiorly and inferiorly from the foramen. In adults, the sphenopalatine foramen measures roughly 6 mm and 5.5 mm in the vertical and horizontal planes, respectively. Endoscopic landmarks for sphenopalatine foramen localization [2] [9] :

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxillary Nerve -- Nerves -- 2) Pterygopalatine fossa:
retrieved_docs.content: The greater and lesser palatine nerves originate from the inferior surface of pterygopalatine ganglion and pass through the palatine canal. The greater palatine nerve emerges on the oral surface of palate through the greater palatine foramen and travels forward within a groove on the inferior surface of the hard palate. It innervates the mucosa and glands of the hard palate, along with adjacent gingiva. It also communicates with the terminal filaments of the nasopalatine nerve. The lesser palatine nerve emerges on the oral surface through the lesser palatine foramen and travels posteriorly to supply the soft palate, tonsils, and uvula. Nasal nerves:

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Boundaries of the sphenopalatine foramen [4] : Superiorly: the body of the sphenoid Anteriorly: the orbital process of the palatine bone. Posteriorly: the sphenoidal process of the palatine bone. Inferiorly:  the perpendicular plate of the palatine bone. Location [5] [6] [7] : The foramen can be found at the posterior-superior-medial corner of the maxillary sinus, just deep to the attachment of the middle turbinate, but exists in a variety of locations: Class I (35 to 82%): the opening is purely into the superior meatus (e.g., above the middle turbinate) Class II (15 to 56%): opening spans the ethmoidal crest (e.g., at the transition of the middle and superior meatus) Class III (2 to 9%): openings exist on both sides of the ethmoidal crest. retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Palatine Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The greater palatine nerve runs through the greater palatine canal and enters the greater palatine foramen, usually located medial to the third maxillary molars, to innervate the hard palate and palatal gingiva. [4] The lesser palatine nerve descends through the lesser palatine foramen, which is posteromedial to the greater palatine foramen, to innervate the soft palate, uvula, and tonsils. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 80 to 90% of individuals have one sphenopalatine foramen per side, while around 10 to 20% of individuals may possess an accessory sphenopalatine foramen, which is typically located anteroinferior to the sphenopalatine foramen. This arrangement is thought to be dictated by the embryologic development of the sphenopalatine artery, which can be highly variable in terms of its number of branches as well as the location of the branch points. Nevertheless, no variables or characteristics show any association with the presence of an accessory sphenopalatine foramen. Contents of the sphenopalatine foramen [1] [8] [11] :

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: After exiting the cranium via the foramen rotundum, the maxillary nerve enters the pterygopalatine fossa and gives rise to multiple branches. Sensory input to maxillary structures is conveyed by the infraorbital (ION), posterior superior alveolar (PSA), middle superior alveolar (MSA), anterior superior alveolar (ASA), palatine, and nasopalatine (NPN) nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Infratemporal Fossa -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of the infratemporal fossa is formed by osseous boundaries located inferiorly and medially to the zygomatic arch. The shape of the infratemporal fossa can be loosely described as an inverted square pyramid containing directional boundaries that delineate its area. Anteriorly, the boundary is formed by the posterolateral surface of the maxillary sinus. The ramus of the mandible forms the lateral boundary while the medial pterygoid and tensor veli palatini muscles form the medial border. The medial border of the infratemporal fossa is continuous with the pterygopalatine fossa. The medial pterygoid muscle attachment to the mandible also forms the inferior border. The lateral pterygoid muscle is located anteromedially to the infratemporal fossa. The posterior boundary of the infratemporal fossa is broadly defined but includes the styloid process and the tympanic part of the temporal bone. Since it an anatomical landmark in the head and neck, the infratemporal fossa functions to house and protect a wide variety of nerves and vasculature that course through the space. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Maxillary Artery
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary branches into the descending palatine artery that then travels through the pterygopalatine fossa down the palatine canal and then branches into the greater and lesser palatine arteries. The greater palatine artery then enters the greater palatine foramen on the posterior aspect of the palate before traversing the palate anteriorly to enter the nasal cavity via the incisive canal. It supplies the septum and the floor of the nasal cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Palatine bone: The palatine bone derives from neural crest cells and forms around the ninth week of embryologic development as part of secondary palate formation.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929

Excision is also the method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” and it is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating a relationship between excisional techniques and nasal aesthetic surgery. Within the hierarchical structure of clinical actions, the group labeled “Clinical actions A‑G” is a child of Excision, making Excision the parent concept for these actions. Conversely, certain spinal operations are explicitly excluded from the Excision category: laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) at lumbar, sacral, or thoracic levels (one or two vertebral segments) are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

Within the procedural taxonomy, Excision serves as a parent category for “Clinical actions A – G,” indicating that these clinical actions are sub‑categories of the broader excision concept. At the same time, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision grouping; these include laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, thoracic, or sacral regions, as well as multisegment laminectomy (> 2 vertebral segments) in the cervical and thoracic regions. This delineation clarifies that such laminectomy techniques are not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region.
